examples.title: PCA from Scratch using NumPy
examples.description: Implementation of PCA algorithm without using sklearn, demonstrating eigen decomposition approach
examples.code:
import numpy as np

def pca_from_scratch(X, n_components=None, variance_threshold=0.95):
    # Step 1: Standardize the data
    X_mean = np.mean(X, axis=0)
    X_std = np.std(X, axis=0)
    X_standardized = (X - X_mean) / X_std
    
    # Step 2: Compute covariance matrix
    n_samples = X_standardized.shape[0]
    cov_matrix = np.cov(X_standardized.T)  # Use .T for correct dimensions
    
    # Step 3: Eigen decomposition
    eigenvalues, eigenvectors = np.linalg.eig(cov_matrix)
    
    # Make eigenvalues real (complex can occur numerically)
    eigenvalues = np.real(eigenvalues)
    eigenvectors = np.real(eigenvectors)
    
    # Sort in descending order
    sorted_indices = np.argsort(eigenvalues)[::-1]
    eigenvalues = eigenvalues[sorted_indices]
    eigenvectors = eigenvectors[:, sorted_indices]
    
    # Step 4: Determine number of components
    if n_components is None:
        total_variance = np.sum(eigenvalues)
        explained_ratio = eigenvalues / total_variance
        cumulative_ratio = np.cumsum(explained_ratio)
        n_components = np.argmax(cumulative_ratio >= variance_threshold) + 1
    
    # Select top n_components eigenvectors
    principal_components = eigenvectors[:, :n_components]
    
    # Step 5: Transform data
    X_reduced = np.dot(X_standardized, principal_components)
    
    return X_reduced, principal_components, eigenvalues[:n_components]

# Example usage
# X_reduced, components, ev = pca_from_scratch(data, variance_threshold=0.95)
examples.explanation: This code implements PCA manually to show each step: standardization, covariance matrix computation, eigen decomposition, component selection based on variance threshold, and final transformation.

examples.title: PCA using Scikit-learn
examples.description: Standard PCA implementation using sklearn library with variance analysis
examples.code:
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
import numpy as np

# Assume X is your data matrix (n_samples, n_features)

# Step 1: Standardize the data
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# Step 2: Apply PCA
# Option A: Specify number of components
pca = PCA(n_components=0.95)  # Retain 95% variance
X_reduced = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)

# Option B: Specify exact number of components
pca = PCA(n_components=2)  # Reduce to 2 dimensions
X_2d = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)

# Analysis outputs
print("Explained variance ratio:", pca.explained_variance_ratio_)
print("Cumulative variance:", np.cumsum(pca.explained_variance_ratio_))
print("Principal components shape:", pca.components_.shape)

# Reconstruction (for visualization of information loss)
X_reconstructed = pca.inverse_transform(X_2d)
reconstruction_error = np.mean((X_scaled - X_reconstructed) ** 2)

# Incremental PCA for large datasets
from sklearn.decomposition import IncrementalPCA
ipca = IncrementalPCA(n_components=100)
for batch in np.array_split(X_scaled, 10):  # Process in batches
    ipca.partial_fit(batch)
X_reduced_large = ipca.transform(X_scaled)
examples.explanation: Demonstrates practical PCA usage with sklearn including variance-based component selection, reconstruction error calculation, and incremental PCA for large datasets.
examples.timeComplexity: O(d²n + d³) handled internally by sklearn

examples.title: Kernel PCA Implementation
examples.description: Implementation showing nonlinear dimensionality reduction using kernel trick
examples.code:
from sklearn.decomposition import KernelPCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# Standardize data first
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# Linear kernel (same as standard PCA)
kpca_linear = KernelPCA(n_components=2, kernel='linear')
X_linear = kpca_linear.fit_transform(X_scaled)

# RBF (Gaussian) kernel - good for nonlinear data
kpca_rbf = KernelPCA(n_components=2, kernel='rbf', gamma=0.1)
X_rbf = kpca_rbf.fit_transform(X_scaled)

# Polynomial kernel
kpca_poly = KernelPCA(n_components=2, kernel='poly', degree=3, coef0=1)
X_poly = kpca_poly.fit_transform(X_scaled)

# Selecting gamma for RBF kernel
# gamma: inverse of radius of influence of samples
# Higher gamma = more local, lower = more global
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV
from sklearn.decomposition import KernelPCA
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline

# Grid search for optimal gamma
param_grid = {'kpca__gamma': [0.01, 0.1, 1, 10]}
pipeline = Pipeline([('kpca', KernelPCA(kernel='rbf', n_components=2))])
grid_search = GridSearchCV(pipeline, param_grid, cv=3)
grid_search.fit(X_scaled)
examples.explanation: Shows how Kernel PCA extends standard PCA to capture nonlinear relationships using different kernel functions: RBF (Gaussian) and polynomial.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n²d + n³) for RBF kernel, higher than linear PCA

examples.title: PCA for Data Visualization
examples.description: Complete example showing PCA-based 2D visualization of high-dimensional data
examples.code:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris

# Load example dataset
iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data
y = iris.target
feature_names = iris.feature_names

# Standardize
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# Apply PCA
pca = PCA(n_components=2)
X_pca = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)

# Create visualization
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))

# Plot 1: 2D projection
scatter = axes[0].scatter(X_pca[:, 0], X_pca[:, 1], c=y, cmap='viridis', alpha=0.7)
axes[0].set_xlabel(f'PC1 ({pca.explained_variance_ratio_[0]*100:.1f}% var)')
axes[0].set_ylabel(f'PC2 ({pca.explained_variance_ratio_[1]*100:.1f}% var)')
axes[0].set_title('PCA: 2D Projection of Iris Dataset')
plt.colorbar(scatter, ax=axes[0], label='Class')

# Plot 2: Explained variance
components = range(1, len(pca.explained_variance_ratio_) + 1)
cumulative = np.cumsum(pca.explained_variance_ratio_)
axes[1].bar(components, pca.explained_variance_ratio_, alpha=0.7, label='Individual')
axes[1].plot(components, cumulative, 'ro-', label='Cumulative')
axes[1].axhline(y=0.95, color='g', linestyle='--', label='95% threshold')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Principal Component')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Explained Variance Ratio')
axes[1].set_title('Scree Plot: Variance Explained')
axes[1].legend()

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Feature importance (component loadings)
loadings = pd.DataFrame(
    pca.components_.T,
    columns=['PC1', 'PC2'],
    index=feature_names
)
print("Feature Loadings:\n", loadings)
examples.explanation: Complete visualization example showing 2D projection of iris dataset, scree plot, and feature loadings (how each original feature contributes to principal components).
